description_long: This bonsai features a thick, textured trunk with pronounced roots that spread outward, giving it a sturdy and ancient appearance. The trunk rises vertically before branching out into several main limbs, each supporting a layered canopy of green foliage. The leaves are arranged in dense, rounded clusters, creating a tiered effect that is characteristic of traditional bonsai styling. The foliage is lush and covers the upper and mid-sections of the tree, while the lower branches are more sparsely populated. The bark of the trunk is rough and brown, contrasting with the vibrant green of the leaves. The overall silhouette is balanced and visually striking, making it a focal point suitable for both indoor and outdoor urban environments.
description: A robust bonsai with a thick, textured trunk and lush, tiered green foliage. The tree's layered branches and dense leaves create a striking, balanced silhouette.
description_view_0: Front view shows the thick trunk, prominent roots, and dense upper foliage with a clear tiered structure.
description_view_1: This view highlights the vertical rise of the trunk and the symmetrical spread of the upper branches and leaves.
description_view_2: The side view reveals the layered arrangement of the foliage and the outward-reaching roots at the base.
description_view_3: This angle emphasizes the curvature of the trunk and the distribution of foliage, with one lower branch more visible.
attribute_pot_type: No pot is visible; the bonsai appears to be displayed without a container, with exposed roots.
attribute_trunk_curvature: The trunk is mostly straight with a slight natural curve and some outward flaring at the base, resembling an informal upright style.
attribute_branch_arrangement: Branches are layered and radiate outward in a tiered pattern, with dense clusters of leaves at each level and some asymmetry in lower branches.
attribute_leaf_density: The foliage is dense and lush, especially in the upper and mid-sections, with some sparser areas on the lower branches.
